bibliographic.citation: Israel Gollancz, ed., St. Erkenwald (Bishop of London 675-693): An Alliterative Poem, Written about 1386, Narrating a Miracle Wrought by the Bishop in St. Paul's Cathedral, Select Early English Poems 4 (London: Humphrey Milford, Oxford University Press, 1922).
bibliographic.title: St. Erkenwald (Bishop of London 675-693): An Alliterative Poem, Written about 1386, Narrating a Miracle Wrought by the Bishop in St. Paul's Cathedral

entities.label: Gollancz 1922 edition of Saint Erkenwald
notes: Gollancz says the edition is based on a fresh transcript of Harley MS 2250 and records deviations from the manuscript in textual and general notes.
Its quatrain arrangement is editorial; square brackets and obeli indicate emendation, and contraction expansions reflect Gollancz's judgement.
The title's date 'about 1386' is Gollancz's conjecture, not an internally dated fact.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1922, before the current United States public-domain cutoff for published works.
rights.component: Gollancz's 1922 edition, transcription and editorial matter

access.accessMethod: Purchase the Liverpool University Press edition or consult it through JSTOR or a holding library. No authorized complete public download was verified.

access.format: Printed scholarly edition and JSTOR digital edition

rights.basis: This is a modern scholarly edition. No open license for the complete edition was found on the verified JSTOR, publisher/distributor, DIMEV, or review records.
rights.component: Turville-Petre's 2024 critical text, translation, introduction, annotations, glossary, and other editorial matter
rights.jurisdiction: Applicable copyright law and provider terms
notes: The public-domain status of the medieval poem does not place the edition's modern editorial expression or translation in the public domain.

bibliographic.citation: Thorlac Turville-Petre, ed., St Erkenwald: A Critical Edition, Exeter Medieval Texts and Studies (Liverpool: Liverpool University Press, 2024).
bibliographic.title: St Erkenwald: A Critical Edition

entities.label: Turville-Petre 2024 critical edition of Saint Erkenwald
notes: DIMEV v1.0 cites this edition as Turville-Petre2024. The Medieval Review describes a facing-page Middle English text and modern English prose translation with introduction, annotations, manuscript gloss appendix, glossary, and index.
The complete edition was not publicly downloadable or locally cached. It is recorded as collateral current scholarship and is not inserted into the verified OTA-to-Gollancz primary transmission path.

rights.basis: This is a modern scholarly edition. No open license for the complete edition was found on the verified JSTOR, publisher/distributor, DIMEV, or review records.

rights.jurisdiction: Applicable copyright law and provider terms
notes: The public-domain status of the medieval poem does not place the edition's modern editorial expression or translation in the public domain.

bibliographic.title: Altenglische Legenden, Neue Folge

entities.label: Horstmann 1881 edition of Saint Erkenwald
notes: DIMEV cites the textual edition at pages 266-274; printed page 265 supplies Horstmann's heading and German summary.
This is a predecessor that Gollancz consulted, not the immediate source of OTA 0053.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1881, before the current United States public-domain cutoff for published works.
rights.component: Horstmann's 1881 edition, transcription and editorial matter

access.accessMethod: Open the exact BL Archives and Manuscripts record.

rights.basis: The exact record states that British Library catalog text is available under a CC BY license except where otherwise stated.
rights.component: British Library catalogue record prose
notes: This catalog-text license does not establish rights in manuscript photographs.

access.accessMethod: Use the BL collection-item request form with shelfmark Harley MS 2250. The manuscript must be consulted in London; the record requires a letter of introduction and Reader Pass conditions apply.

Submit the British Library Imaging Services online order for colour photography of the complete physical Harley MS 2250, not only the poem leaves. Quote the shelfmark and request bindings, covers, pastedowns, flyleaves, every recto and verso, inserted leaves, unfoliated matter, blanks, and stubs where reproducible; map Saint Erkenwald separately to fols. 72v-75v.
access.contact: licensing@bl.uk for reproduction/licensing information; permissions@bl.uk for written permission under the Imaging Services terms
access.format: Colour photography of the original manuscript; ordinary scans are available only from surrogates; standard delivery is electronic

localCopies.label: British Library digitisation and reproduction services price list (2025/26 rates)

notes: The 2025/26 rate card lists photographic TIFF at £27.70 for the first image and £7.25 for each subsequent same-item image, or £58.19 per bespoke image; a complete-codex order requires an item-specific quotation.
The service says recto may be abbreviated r or a and verso v or b, confirming that CME's 72b-75b milestones map to 72v-75v rather than to a different foliation.
The requested full object includes substantially more than the target poem. Folio 75v is a shared boundary because the following Life of St John the Baptist begins on the same leaf.

Standard processing is stated as 30 working days after payment, but large or difficult orders can require different timing, handling, and prices. Supply of files does not itself authorize repository redistribution or publication.

rights.basis: The service distinguishes ordering and supply from reuse permission. Its January 2026 terms place third-party-rights clearance on the customer and state that supplied material must not be copied or reproduced without British Library written permission, which may involve additional fees.
rights.component: Any reference or reproduction photographs supplied through British Library Imaging Services
rights.jurisdiction: British Library Imaging Services terms and applicable copyright law
notes: The service page directs reproduction enquiries to licensing@bl.uk; the special terms direct written-permission enquiries to permissions@bl.uk. Obtain item-specific written terms for Git redistribution and commercial or noncommercial print-on-demand publication before publishing supplied images.

dateStatements.label: catalog date
dateStatements.notes: John Watton dated the Speculum Christiani on f. 64v to 1477, and the BL catalog says fols. 65-83 are probably in the same hand; this connection does not make every text's copying date independently certain.
dateStatements.value: second half of the fifteenth century

entities.description: The sole surviving manuscript witness of Saint Erkenwald, at fols. 72v-75v within a paper religious miscellany.

holding.notes: Saint Erkenwald occupies fols. 72v-75v in the current BL catalog and DIMEV.

entities.label: British Library Harley MS 2250
notes: The legacy a/b notation is the same leaf-side convention as modern r/v notation: 72b is 72v, and the CME milestones run through 75b/75v. Gollancz's preface and OTA repeat an endpoint of 75a, but Gollancz's poem itself marks [F. 75b] before line 341; current BL and DIMEV records likewise end the work at 75v. The 75a endpoint is therefore preserved as a source-layer error, not treated as evidence of refoliation.
The manuscript title recorded by DIMEV is De Sancto Erkenwaldo Episcopo.
The following Life of St John the Baptist begins on f. 75v, so Saint Erkenwald's final leaf is a shared manuscript boundary.

physicalDescription.extent: 108 folios plus inserted and unfoliated leaves
notes: Gothic cursive script with small red initials.
The current record localizes fols. 1r-65v to Cheshire and says fols. 65-83 are probably in the same hand as John Watton's dated 1477 section.
DIMEV/eLALME assigns Saint Erkenwald at fols. 72v-75v to Linguistic Profile 419, Cheshire.

rights.basis: The poem and manuscript were created centuries before modern copyright. This does not govern modern photographs, editions, transcriptions, interfaces or provider terms.
rights.component: Underlying medieval Middle English poem and manuscript textual content

rights.basis: The exact record states that British Library catalog text is available under a CC BY license except where otherwise stated.

The v1.0 data also identifies the work's eLALME profile and supplies a bibliography of editions through Turville-Petre 2024.

next_steps: Treat a/b and r/v as equivalent side notation: 72b=72v and 75b=75v.
Preserve Gollancz/OTA's 75a endpoint as a documented source-layer error because Gollancz's poem, CME, BL and DIMEV support 75b/75v.
When images return or are supplied, verify the line-to-folio transitions at CME milestones 72b, 73a, 73b, 74a, 74b, 75a and 75b without reopening the already established a/b crosswalk.
openQuestions.question: Can restored manuscript images confirm the exact placement of every internal CME folio milestone against the current BL pencil foliation?

next_steps: Treat Gollancz's 1386 date and London-author argument as attributed conjectures rather than facts.
Consult the complete Turville-Petre 2024 edition, beyond its verified bibliographic record and independent review, before adopting its Cheshire audience, patron or author profile as fact.
Keep the Gawain/Pearl Poet, Ralph Strode and John Massey of Cotton claims as low-confidence attribution history unless stronger evidence or consensus emerges.
Keep witness dialect, literary setting and place of composition as separate assertions.
openQuestions.question: Can the poem's composition date, place or author be narrowed beyond a late-fourteenth- or early-fifteenth-century anonymous Northwest Midland work associated with London?

next_steps: Recheck the exact LARK object and ask Imaging Services whether a complete preservation image set already exists; do not infer whole-codex coverage from the identifier alone.

If ordering images, request the whole physical manuscript, including bindings, endleaves, blanks, inserts and both sides of every leaf, and map Saint Erkenwald separately to fols. 72v-75v with the shared f. 75v boundary recorded.

Use the current order form and obtain written terms from licensing@bl.uk and permissions@bl.uk for Git redistribution and commercial or noncommercial print-on-demand use before publishing supplied images.
openQuestions.question: Does the inaccessible legacy BL object contain a complete Harley MS 2250 sequence, and what item-specific supply and reuse terms would govern a commissioned complete-codex facsimile?

next_steps: Do not assume that public-domain status of the medieval poem or Gollancz edition removes the noncommercial condition from copied OTA/U-M expression.
Seek permission or prepare a demonstrably independent transcription from a public-domain edition or manuscript facsimile for commercial distribution.
Document which textual layer each generated artifact actually uses.
openQuestions.question: How should future commercially usable corpus outputs separate public-domain source text from the CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 OTA/U-M encoding and the repository's broader output-license statement?

next_steps: Inspect CME repository history and surviving U-M export documentation.
Add a copied_from relation only if an exact byte-level transfer can be documented.
openQuestions.question: What exact U-M release, download event and repository-side transformation produced the pinned CME90053 XML blob?

reviewNotes: The primary transmission path is artifact -> U-M CME encoding -> OTA 0053 -> Gollancz 1922 -> Harley MS 2250; Horstmann 1881 is a consulted predecessor, not the immediate source of OTA or U-M.
The unique medieval witness is kept distinct from the abstract poem, printed editions, digital encodings, catalog descriptions and scans.

Legacy a/b and current r/v are equivalent leaf-side notation. The only substantive locator conflict is Gollancz/OTA's 75a endpoint, preserved as a source-layer error against Gollancz's own 75b milestone and the CME/BL/DIMEV endpoint 75b/75v.

Verified public supporting downloads include complete Gollancz and Horstmann scans, the complete DIMEV release, OTA files, Schustereder's article, Gordon's CC BY 4.0 article and the BL rate card. No manuscript images were cached because the exact legacy BL object currently reports that its images are unavailable.

rights.component: DIMEV v1.0 data, documentation and record 706

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a version of University of Michigan CME item CME90053, whose 2023 header says that ME Compendium marked up Oxford Text Archive item 0053 and checked it against Israel Gollancz's 1922 edition. OTA 0053 states directly that it was transcribed from Gollancz. Gollancz based his edition on a fresh transcript of the sole surviving witness, British Library Harley MS 2250, fols. 72v-75v, while consulting Carl Horstmann's 1881 predecessor edition. Complete public scans of both printed editions and the complete DIMEV v1.0 release are cached locally. The exact BL catalogue links an unavailable legacy image object, but its published extent cannot currently be verified; complete-codex photography can instead be requested from Imaging Services subject to condition, price, and reuse permission.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; probably slightly after 1386 and no later than 1420; sole witness copied in the second half of the fifteenth century
catalogSummary.region: England; sole-witness language localized to Cheshire (eLALME LP 419); London and St Paul's Cathedral are the setting, not a proved composition place
catalogSummary.title: Saint Erkenwald

catalogingSubject.label: Saint Erkenwald
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous 352-line Middle English alliterative poem conventionally called Saint Erkenwald. It survives uniquely in British Library Harley MS 2250, fols. 72v-75v, and narrates the bishop's miraculous encounter with a just pagan judge found beneath St Paul's Cathedral. CME90053 encodes the whole poem through the OTA and Gollancz textual chain. The subject is not Erkenwald's complete historical biography, the entire Harley manuscript, Gollancz's preface and notes, or the broader works sometimes attributed to the Gawain/Pearl Poet.

contentParts.label: U-M editorial opener and argument

locators.value: BODY/OPENER and BODY/ARGUMENT before DIV1 TYPE='poem'

notes: This retained encoding-layer paratext is modern English. It does not change the abstract medieval work's Middle English language or verse form.
contentParts.summary: A modern-English byline and February 2023 dateline identify the OTA/Gollancz basis, followed by an argument explaining lowercase transcription, thorn conversion, removed punctuation, added folio and line references, and treatment of Gollancz's emendations and manuscript readings.

contentParts.label: U-M navigation: [Prologue]
notes: The opening CME milestone 72b is equivalent to 72v; a/b and r/v are alternate leaf-side abbreviations.

Gollancz prints a bracketed [PROLOGUE] label, and CME ends its prologue division at line 33. Schustereder instead describes the first 32 lines as the historical introduction and reports no manuscript division at line 33; the navigation label is not cataloged as authorial manuscript structure.
contentParts.summary: CME90053 groups lines 1-33 under [PROLOGUE]. The passage recalls the conversion of England and London, the rededication of pagan temples, London's identity as New Troy and Erkenwald's episcopate at St Paul's.

contentParts.label: U-M navigation: Fit I

locators.value: approximately ff. 72v-74r, inferred from CME milestones 72b-74a

notes: The folio span follows the established a=recto and b=verso crosswalk; exact line placement can be visually checked if images become available.
Schustereder reports no manuscript capital or other division at line 34. 'Fit I' is retained as U-M navigation rather than asserted as manuscript structure.
contentParts.summary: CME90053 groups lines 34-176 as Fit I. Builders find the carved marble tomb and an undecayed crowned body; London crowds to the discovery; Erkenwald returns from Essex, celebrates mass and approaches the unknown corpse after written searches fail.

contentParts.label: U-M navigation: Fit II

locators.value: approximately ff. 74r-75v, inferred from CME milestones 74a-75b

notes: Line 177 coincides with the manuscript's second reported major capital, but the 'Fit II' label remains U-M navigation rather than a transcribed manuscript heading. The folio span follows the established a/r and b/v crosswalk and can be visually checked if images become available.
Saint Erkenwald ends on f. 75v, where the following Life of St John the Baptist also begins; this is a shared physical boundary.
contentParts.summary: CME90053 groups lines 177-352 as Fit II. The corpse recounts a life of impartial pagan justice and exclusion from Christian salvation; Erkenwald longs to baptize him; a tear falls during the baptismal formula, the soul is released, the body turns to dust and the city processes with its bells ringing.

assertion.notes: Schustereder reports Peterson's view that linguistic evidence points probably slightly later than 1386 and no later than 1420. Only the explicit upper bound is normalized; 'slightly later' is not converted into an invented exact lower year.

dateStatements.display: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; probably slightly after 1386 and no later than 1420

assertion.notes: Gollancz says the poem supplies no internal date evidence and infers 1386 from renewed observance of Erkenwald's feast. It must not be exposed as an exact composition date.
The BL record dates the codex 1450-1499 and says fols. 65-83 are probably in the hand associated with John Watton's 1477 colophon.

dateStatements.display: Second half of the fifteenth century (sole witness; connected with a 1477 scribe)

assertion.notes: Horstmann's complete text is the earliest edition identified by the checked scholarship.

extent.description: One complete 352-line Middle English verse work plus one modern-English U-M editorial-framing unit. The poem has no LG verse-group elements. Its three DIV2 containers ([Prologue] and two fits) are counted as encoding-level navigation sections rather than three separate medieval works or a complete manuscript hierarchy.

formStatements.description: A 352-line unrhymed alliterative poem in long lines; CME90053 encodes each line as an L element.

assertion.notes: DIMEV and the BL catalog classify the item as a life of Saint Erkenwald, though the poem narrates one miracle rather than a cradle-to-grave biography.
The resurrected judge functions as an ethical exemplar of impartial justice; 'exemplum' is a secondary generic facet, not a replacement for miracle narrative or hagiography.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The Latin rubric/title and modern-English U-M framing do not make the substantively Middle English poem multilingual for whole-work filtering.
The abstract medieval poem is distinct from the historical Saint Erkenwald, his full life, Harley MS 2250 as a physical codex, Gollancz's 1922 edition, OTA 0053 and CME90053.

Anonymous authorship is the default. The Gawain/Pearl Poet association, Gollancz's Ralph Strode speculation and Peterson's John Massey of Cotton candidate are retained only as low-confidence attribution history.

The composition statement normalizes only the sourced upper bound of 1420. Schustereder's 'probably slightly later than 1386' report is preserved in prose rather than converted into an invented exact lower year; Gollancz's about-1386 claim is separately modeled as a low-confidence conjecture.
Cheshire/eLALME LP 419 describes the language of the sole surviving witness at fols. 72v-75v. It does not prove an authorial origin or composition place.

Legacy a/b and current r/v are equivalent side notation. The Gollancz/OTA 75a endpoint is retained as a source-layer error because Gollancz's own [F. 75b] marker, CME, BL and DIMEV establish 75b/75v; f. 75v is shared with the following Life of St John the Baptist.
The modern-English U-M opener and argument are modeled as encoding-layer prose paratext; discovery language and form remain Middle English and verse for the abstract medieval work.

Complete public scans of the Gollancz and Horstmann editions, OTA files, DIMEV v1.0 archive, Schustereder and Gordon articles, and the BL price list are cached under source-cache/CME90053. The manuscript images remain uncached because the legacy BL object is unavailable and its published extent cannot be verified.

next_steps: Consult the complete Turville-Petre 2024 edition beyond its verified record and independent review before adopting its Cheshire audience, patron or author profile as fact.
Keep Gollancz's 1386 date and London-author argument, his Ralph Strode speculation, Peterson's John Massey of Cotton candidate, and Gawain/Pearl Poet association attributed and qualified.
Do not convert work-specific witness dialect into a certain authorial birthplace or composition place.
openQuestions.question: Can current scholarship narrow the poem's composition date, place or authorship beyond an anonymous late-fourteenth- or early-fifteenth-century alliterative work with Northwest Midland/Cheshire language and a London setting?

next_steps: Verify the two reported capitals and surrounding layout against complete Harley MS 2250 images when available.
Continue to model Gollancz's quatrains and [PROLOGUE] label and U-M's lines 1-33, 34-176 and 177-352 divisions as editorial/encoding navigation.
Preserve the distinction between Schustereder's 32-line historical introduction and U-M's 33-line [Prologue] division.
openQuestions.question: Can manuscript images visually confirm the reported capitals at lines 1 and 177 and the absence of further codicological division marks?

next_steps: Treat a=recto and b=verso as established equivalent notation, following the current BL imaging guidance.
Preserve Gollancz/OTA's 75a endpoint as a source-layer error because Gollancz's poem, CME, BL and DIMEV support 75b/75v.
Use images only to verify exact line-to-folio transitions and the shared f. 75v boundary, not to reopen the a/b crosswalk without contrary evidence.
openQuestions.question: Can restored or commissioned images confirm the exact line placement of CME's internal folio milestones against the current BL pencil foliation?

assertion.notes: This statement is specific to fols. 72v-75v and eLALME LP 419; other sections of Harley MS 2250 have other profiles.

scope.description: Language of the poem in the sole surviving manuscript, not a proved authorial place of composition

assertion.notes: The current edition challenges the conventional 'London poem' label and argues for a Cheshire audience and patronal setting. The reviewed claim does not establish a certain authorial birthplace or exact place of composition.

scope.description: Proposed audience and patronal context in Turville-Petre's 2024 edition, as reported by Battles
The action centers on St Paul's Cathedral in London, imagined also as ancient New Troy.

assertion.notes: The strong London association does not prove that the poem was composed in London.

scope.description: Association with the cult of Saint Erkenwald and St Paul's Cathedral

assertion.notes: No source identifies a securely known author.
Similarities with Pearl, Patience, Cleanness and Sir Gawain and the Green Knight have prompted attribution, but common authorship remains unresolved and is not the catalog default.
Gollancz says one 'fondly plays with the fancy' that Ralph Strode was concerned and warns that theorizing is dangerous without clear evidence. This historical speculation is not the catalog default.
Schustereder reports Peterson's conclusion that John Massey of Cotton met his proposed authorship criteria, while also stating that the associated authorship discussion had reached no consensus.

scope.description: Editor of the 1922 immediate source edition
Fresh transcript of Harley MS 2250, fols. 72v-75v, underlying the 1922 edition
Editor of the 1881 first known printed edition, a predecessor consulted by Gollancz
Editor and translator of the collateral 2024 current critical edition

assertion.notes: Gordon identifies Erkenwald's command at lines 179-184 as a formal conjuration and contextualizes it through medieval exorcism, discernment and apotropaic practice.

subjects.label: Conjuration, exorcism, and discernment of the dead

summaries.value: During rebuilding at St Paul's Cathedral, workers discover an undeciphered marble tomb containing the incorrupt body of a richly dressed man. Bishop Erkenwald summons the corpse to speak. It identifies itself as a just pagan judge of ancient New Troy who was honored for impartial judgment but remained excluded from salvation because he had never received Christian baptism. As Erkenwald utters the Trinitarian baptismal formula, his tears fall on the corpse and become baptismal water; the released soul enters heaven, the body decays, and London responds with procession and bells.

After a modern-English U-M opener and argument, CME90053 supplies three navigation divisions: [Prologue], lines 1-33, on London, conversion and the rebuilding of St Paul's; Fit I, lines 34-176, on discovery of the tomb, the incorrupt body and Erkenwald's arrival; and Fit II, lines 177-352, on the pagan judge's testimony, salvation without baptism, Erkenwald's tears, the soul's release and the closing procession. These are encoding/editorial divisions, not a complete manuscript section hierarchy. Scholarship describes the first 32 lines as the historical introduction and reports manuscript capitals only at lines 1 and 177.

assertion.notes: The expanded modern spelling used by CME90053 and the principal source edition.
DIMEV record 706 identifies this as the manuscript rubric/title.
The shorter Latin form appears as the CME XML work head and in the OTA source description.
DIMEV preserves the manuscript reading 'sithen'. CME90053 instead places Gollancz's emended 'tyme' in the line and adds '[MS: sythen]'.

titles.value: At London in England not full long sithen

assertion.notes: The source spelling of line 352 as encoded in CME90053; DIMEV confirms the same explicit with manuscript characters.

titles.value: And alle the belles in the burghe beryd at ones

work.summary: During rebuilding at St Paul's Cathedral, workers discover an undeciphered marble tomb containing the incorrupt body of a richly dressed man. Bishop Erkenwald summons the corpse to speak. It identifies itself as a just pagan judge of ancient New Troy who was honored for impartial judgment but remained excluded from salvation because he had never received Christian baptism. As Erkenwald utters the Trinitarian baptismal formula, his tears fall on the corpse and become baptismal water; the released soul enters heaven, the body decays, and London responds with procession and bells.
